title: Chapter 35: The Unraveling
content: The video played.
Forty-seven seconds. That was all it took.
The massive LED screens flanking the runway — screens that moments ago had displayed the Thornfield logo in spinning gold — now showed something else entirely. Grainy. Basement-lit. Unmistakable.

Edward Rochester, striding across a concrete floor. A syringe in his hand. A woman on a cot — Bertha, her dark hair fanned across a thin pillow, her wrists bruised where restraints had been. Edward's hand on her arm. The needle going in. Her body going limp.
The audio was worse. A low, desperate keening that cut off mid-note. Edward's voice, flat and clinical: *"She needs another dose. Keep her sedated until after the IPO."*
Someone in the audience screamed.

Jane heard it from a distance, as though underwater. The scream was real — a woman in the third row, her hand over her mouth, her champagne flute shattering on the glass floor. But Jane's body had already gone somewhere else. Somewhere cold and clear and absolutely certain.
The video wasn't done.

The basement footage dissolved into documents. Financial records scrolling across the screens — shell companies in Luxembourg and Malta, wire transfers routed through Cyprus, the Seychelles account numbered and named and tied, with devastating clarity, to Dr. Henri Fournier's private clinic. Payment after payment. Date after date. Each one corresponding to a period when Bertha Mason had been "receiving treatment." Each one proof that her confinement was purchased, not prescribed.

Then: another video. A factory. Somewhere in Southeast Asia — the scrolling geocode said Vietnam. Children's hands sorting fabric. No masks. No ventilation. Vats of dye chemicals emitting fumes that made the camera lens fog. A supervisor with a clipboard. A Thornfield subcontractor logo on the wall.
Four hundred million people were watching.

Jane could feel it — the simultaneous intake of breath across time zones, across continents, across the livestream comments that had gone from heart emojis to exclamation marks to a single, deafening word repeating like a pulse: *NO. NO. NO. NO.*

Edward's face appeared on the big screen in a cutaway. The camera had been trained on him the whole time. The visionary CEO. The Renaissance man of fashion. But the man on the screen wasn't visionary anymore. His jaw was locked. His eyes were wide, the pupils shrunk to pinpoints. Sweat beaded at his temples. He looked — for the first time in the decade Jane had known him — like exactly what he was.
A man caught. A man exposed.
"Cut the feed! Cut it NOW!"

Edward's voice cracked across the runway like a whip. He spun toward the security team stationed at the wings — four men in black, earpieces glinting, hands already moving to their belts. "Kill the screens! Kill the stream! NOW!"
The head of security, a stocky man named Cortez, pressed his earpiece and spoke rapidly. A pause. Another attempt. His face changed.
"Sir, we can't."
"What do you mean you can't?"
"The system's been bypassed. There's a back door in the server — it's running independent of venue control. Someone hardcoded the playback sequence."

Edward's composure fractured. Jane watched it happen — watched the architecture of him buckle, watched the careful mask of control that had held for years develop a fault line. His hand went to his temple. His breathing quickened. He was calculating, still calculating, the way he always calculated: variables, leverages, exits.
But before he could calculate a way out, every phone in the room buzzed.
Simultaneously.

Four hundred devices — influencers, editors, buyers, shareholders, journalists — all vibrating in unison. The Thornfield app, the one every attendee had downloaded for their digital invitation and seat assignment, had just pushed a notification. An air-drop. A data packet.
Jane's own phone lit up in her clutch. She didn't need to look. She knew what it contained. She'd helped compile it.

The basement footage. The financial documents. The supply chain video. Bertha's medical records. Fournier's invoices. The competency declaration. Everything. Delivered directly into 400 palms, bypassing every gatekeeper, every firewall, every fixer Edward Rochester had ever hired.
You could seize a server. You could cut a feed. You could compel silence with NDAs and settlements and the weight of a billion-euro empire.
But you couldn't confiscate 400 phones. You couldn't un-send what had already been received. You couldn't stop the screenshot, the forward, the download, the share.
Edward's empire of control — the careful, meticulous, absolute control under which he had governed every person and every narrative in his orbit — had just discovered its fatal flaw.
You can't control the truth once it's free.
Jane moved.

Her fingers found the hidden zipper at her waist — the cool metal teeth, the small pull that Grace had sewn into the oatmeal sheath three days ago in the atelier, her hands steady, her face revealing nothing. Jane had felt it every time she'd worn the shell dress: that small, secret ridge against her hip. A promise. A doorway.

One smooth pull. The zipper tracked from hip to hem, and the oatmeal shell loosened, gaped, and fell away from her body like a chrysalis splitting open. Jane stepped out of it — out of the beige, the safe, the appropriate, the *chosen for her* — and stood on the glass runway in the crimson dress.

The dress blazed under the LED lights. Deep, arterial red. Tea-stained silk that caught the light like wine catching sun. The bodice fitted close, the skirt falling to her ankles in a clean line that Grace had cut with her own hands, that Bertha had sketched in the margins of a notebook years ago, that Jane had modified in the atelier at midnight with a needle and a quiet fury.
The audience gasped.

Not the polite gasp of a fashion show — the *other* kind. The kind that happens when a woman steps out of a cage and everyone sees the door was open the whole time. The cameras pivoted. Every phone in the venue rose, capturing the moment: the fiancée in red, standing on the glass runway over the water, standing *in* the truth.

Jane felt the cool air on her bare arms where the oatmeal sleeves had been. Felt the weight of the silk against her skin, heavy and real. Felt the diamond on her left hand — the engagement ring, the chain he'd bought to bind her — and knew, with absolute clarity, that her hands were never meant for wearing chains.
*He bought me the diamond to chain my hands. Too bad my hands are meant for typing his downfall.*
Edward turned on her.

His face — for the first time in all the years Jane had known him, in all the months of their engagement, in all the careful hours of their life together — was unmasked. Not charming. Not visionary. Not even angry. Something beneath all of that. Something feral. The mask didn't slip; it shattered. She could see the pieces of it in his eyes — the charm, the warmth, the intensity, the *love* — all of it lying in fragments around something raw and cornered and dangerous.
"You."
One word. A whole accusation. A whole indictment. A whole confession.

Jane met his eyes. She did not flinch. She did not look away. She did not offer explanation or apology or the soft, conciliatory thing she'd offered so many times before — the *I'm sorry, I didn't mean to upset you* that had become as reflexive as breathing.
"Me."
Not defensive. Not apologetic. Just: *me.*
The woman you tried to reshape. The woman who wore your cage. The woman who kept the crimson underneath.

The silence between them held for exactly two seconds, but it stretched like taffy, like a held breath, like the space between a lightning strike and the thunder. Around them, the auditorium had devolved into chaos — people standing, shouting, filming, crying — but on the runway, in the glare of the lights, there was only this: a man who had believed himself untouchable, and a woman who had waited for the exact right moment to prove him wrong.

Edward's hand twitched. For a terrible, suspended instant, Jane thought he might grab her — might try to drag her off the runway, into the wings, into the narrative he could still control. His fingers curled. His jaw worked.
And then the backstage curtain moved.
Bertha walked out.

She came through the gap in the black draping the way she'd once walked down a runway — one foot in front of the other, her shoulders back, her chin level. She wore the punk tuxedo jacket. The one with the safety pins glinting along the lapels like tiny silver stars, the exposed seams running down the arms like topographic maps, the HELP code stitched across the back in thread so fine it could only be read up close — and now, with the cameras zooming, four hundred million people were reading it.
Not a ghost. Not a madwoman. A designer. A genius. A survivor.

The audience didn't know who she was — not yet. But Jane saw the shift in the room as Bertha appeared: the recalibration, the sense that something significant had just entered the frame. She was thin — thinner than she should have been, three days of whatever Fournier's clinic had been pumping into her leaving its mark — but her eyes were clear. Her hands were steady. Her spine was a thing of iron.
She walked to the center of the runway and stood beside Jane.
Two women. Two garments. One truth.
Edward looked at Bertha and his face went white.

Not angry-white. Not flushed with fury. The white of a man seeing something impossible. A ghost. A problem he'd solved, standing in front of him in a jacket covered in safety pins. He hadn't seen her in three days. He'd left her in a clinic outside Lyon, drugged and docile and declared incompetent by a physician whose signatures were for sale. He'd thought she was contained.
"Bertha—"
She didn't let him finish.

She turned from him — turned away from Edward Rochester as though he were furniture, as though he were background, as though he were nothing — and faced the audience. Then she walked to the microphone stand that Edward had abandoned minutes ago, when the world was still his and the night was still under his control. She adjusted it with both hands. She leaned in.
"My name is Bertha Mason."
Her voice was clear. Steady. Unhurried. The voice of a woman who had been silenced for five years and had no intention of rushing now.

"I designed every collection Thornfield has released in the last five years. Every garment. Every stitch. The Spring Equinox line. The Meridian collection. The piece you just saw walk down this runway." She paused. Let it land. "He took my name off them and put his own."

The auditorium had gone quiet. Not silent — there was still the murmur of the livestream echo, the rustle of hundreds of people leaning forward — but the kind of hush that falls when the truth arrives and everyone knows it.

"And when I tried to tell the truth — when I went to the board, when I went to the press, when I tried to leave — he locked me in the basement of his château and had me declared mentally incompetent. He told people I was unstable. He told people I was dangerous. He had a doctor inject me with sedatives against my will, and he called it treatment."
She held up the punk jacket. Turned it slowly so the cameras could catch every seam, every pin, every line of coded stitching.
"Here is the evidence. Five years of proof. Stitched into the seams."

The screens — still running the bypass feed — split. Close-up of the jacket on one side. Close-up of Bertha's face on the other. The HELP code magnified until every letter was legible. Then: the backup files. The dated sketches in Bertha's hand. The emails where she'd protested. The medical records from Fournier's clinic, timestamped and signed.
All of it. All of it, live.
The audience erupted.

It wasn't applause. It was something rawer — a wave of sound that hit Jane like a physical force. Some people were on their feet, shouting. Some were crying. Some had their phones raised, already posting, already sharing, already amplifying. The roar of it was immense, human, and filled with something Jane recognized as the particular sound of outrage finding its voice.

The private security team moved — but not toward Jane. Not toward Bertha. They moved toward Edward. Cortez and his men flanked him with the same efficiency they'd used to protect him, but their postures had shifted. They weren't shielding him from the crowd. They were containing him for it.

In the front row, a woman in Chanel was weeping into her scarf. Two seats down, a hedge fund manager was already on his phone — not calling for damage control, but calling his lawyers to get *out*. The IPO underwriters. Jane could see them: three suits near the exit, their faces ashen, their phones pressed to their ears. Canceling. Everything. Now.

At 8:46 PM, Richard Mason's legal filing hit the French court system. Timestamped. Notarized. Criminal complaint against Edward Rochester for unlawful confinement, fraud, and forgery. Attached: 347 pages of evidence.

At 8:47 PM, Helen Burns's story went live. The investigative piece — the one she'd been building for months, the one Jane had fed from the inside, the one that named names and followed money and traced the architecture of Edward's control from the château basement to the boardroom — published simultaneously across three outlets. Headline: *THE GHOST IN THE ATELIER: HOW EDWARD ROCHESTER STOLE A LEGACY AND IMPRISONED THE WOMAN WHO BUILT IT.*
Every claim Bertha made on that runway, Helen's story corroborated.

Jane stood on the glass. She could feel the runway beneath her feet — the slight give of the platform, the coolness seeping through the thin soles of her shoes. She could smell the perfume of 800 people, the sharp tang of adrenaline, the ozone heat of cameras and lights. She could hear it all: the roar, the sobs, the frantic tap-tap-tap of thumbs on glass, the sound of an empire coming apart.

The architecture of Edward Rochester's world wasn't crumbling. Crumbling implied something passive, something slow, something that happened to old buildings left too long in the rain. This was different. This was *being disassembled* — actively, methodically, in real-time, on live television, by the women he thought he'd erased. By the wife he'd locked away. By the fiancée he'd reshaped. By the journalist he'd underestimated. By the brother-in-law he'd dismissed. By every stitch and every document and every hidden code that had been waiting, patient and precise, for exactly this moment.

Edward Rochester stood alone on the runway he'd built on other people's talent. The mask didn't fit anymore. There was nothing left underneath it that could save him — nothing charming, nothing visionary, nothing that could turn this narrative, nothing that could make four hundred million people unsee what they'd seen. He was surrounded by eight hundred witnesses and four hundred million screens, and every single one of them was recording.

Jane looked at him. At this man she'd almost married. At this man whose oatmeal dress she'd just stepped out of. At this man who had looked at her, once, and seen someone he could reshape — and who had never, not once, seen the woman she actually was.

She didn't feel triumph. Triumph was too clean a word for this. What she felt was something older, heavier, more complex: the profound and terrible satisfaction of truth arriving at last, like a thread pulled through fabric, bringing the whole garment apart.
The empire was falling.
The women were standing.
And the world was watching.
